target.title: Fix Feishu card action mention gating
target.body:
Fixes #85450.

## Summary

- Mark Feishu synthetic group card-action callbacks with bot mention metadata when `botOpenId` is known.
- Keep p2p card callbacks unchanged so direct-message callbacks do not gain synthetic mentions.
- Add regression coverage for both the group mention metadata and the p2p no-mention behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu structured card button callbacks in `requireMention: true` group chats were synthesized as group text messages without bot mention metadata, so the existing group activation gate could treat the callback as not addressing the bot and drop it. After this patch, synthetic group card callbacks include a bot mention entry when `botOpenId` is available, while p2p callbacks still omit synthetic mentions.

Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/googlerest/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src`, branch `codex/fix-85450-feishu-card-mention`, commit `2482f7f442 Fix Feishu card action mention gating`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.card-action.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/policy.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local OpenClaw checkout:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /Users/googlerest/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src

Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  102 passed (102)
  Start at  02:24:43
  Duration  3.34s (transform 1.01s, setup 33ms, import 2.34s, tests 843ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: The Feishu card-action regression now asserts that a synthetic group callback has `message.chat_type === "group"` and includes `mentions: [{ key: "mention_bot", id: { open_id: "ou_bot" }, name: "bot" }]`. The same suite also asserts that p2p card callbacks keep `message.mentions` undefined. The broader Feishu bot and policy regression files passed with the same patch. `git diff --check` completed with no output.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Feishu group chat card click because this local environment does not have live Feishu credentials.

target.title: Config: scaffold compaction loop guard settings
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the loop-aware compaction guard plan needed a small, reviewable config/schema landing point before detector/runtime work.
- Why it matters: separating config scaffolding from runtime behavior keeps review narrow and lets follow-up PRs build on a stable config surface.
- What changed: added `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` types, schema validation, conservative default materialization, docs/help/labels, and focused regression tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no detector, no scoring, no runtime hook, no automatic reset behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #48238

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Config docs/schema now expose a reserved `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` block.
- `loadConfig()` materializes `agents.defaults.compaction.guard.enabled = false` when compaction defaults are materialized.
- No runtime compaction behavior changes.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: local host checkout
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): targeted config fixtures under `src/config/*`

### Steps

1. Add `compaction.guard` fields to agent-default types and zod schema.
2. Materialize conservative defaults in `applyCompactionDefaults()` without adding runtime guard logic.
3. Update config docs/help/labels and add focused regression/default tests.
4. Run targeted config test wrapper.

### Expected

- Reserved guard config parses and documents correctly.
- Invalid enum/range values are rejected.
- Defaulted guard shape is present without changing runtime behavior.

### Actual

- Targeted config tests passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: explicit valid `compaction.guard` config, defaulted disabled guard shape, invalid escalation rejection, invalid numeric bounds rejection, docs/help parity.
- Edge cases checked: guard block remains config-only and does not add runtime compaction behavior.
- What you did **not** verify: detector/scorer/runtime follow-up behavior (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commit `206c7b018f045f967b2578e2679f8674a9eb7581` or remove the reserved `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` block from config.
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/*` compaction schema/default files and `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected config parsing/defaulting regressions under `agents.defaults.compaction`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: default materialization under `agents.defaults.compaction` could subtly affect config snapshots/load expectations.
  - Mitigation: focused regression/default tests cover explicit config, defaulted shape, and rejection cases.

target.title: fix(slack): add renderPresentation to channel outbound adapter
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Slack messages with `MessagePresentation` payloads (rich Block Kit blocks) always render as plain text fallback because `slackChannelOutbound` is missing the `renderPresentation` delegate. Additionally, `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` in `outbound-bridge.ts` checks `text` before `presentation`/`interactive`, causing rich payloads to report receipt `kind: "text"` instead of `"card"`.

**Solution**: Add `renderPresentation` to `slackChannelOutbound` that delegates to the already-implemented `slackOutbound.renderPresentation` via the lazy module loader. Fix `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` priority: presentation/interactive → channelData → text.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/slack/src/channel.ts`: +4 — `renderPresentation` delegate on `slackChannelOutbound`
- `extensions/slack/src/channel.test.ts`: +46 — 2 tests verifying adapter exposes renderPresentation and delegates correctly
- `src/channels/message/outbound-bridge.ts`: +13/-3 — receipt kind priority: card before text
- `src/channels/message/outbound-bridge.test.ts`: +67 — 3 tests for card/text/channelData priority

**What did NOT change**:
- `slackOutbound.renderPresentation` in `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.ts` — already implemented, untouched
- Other channel adapters (Discord, Telegram, Matrix, Feishu) — untouched
- Server-side model routing or auth — untouched
- Config, migration, protocol — untouched

Fixes #95440

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Slack `--presentation` payloads never render as Block Kit — always fall through to `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText`. `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` reports `"text"` instead of `"card"` for presentation-bearing payloads.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Repository**: zhangqueping/openclaw, branch `fix/issue-95440` at `c03c533a5`
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Vitest**: 4.1.8

**Root cause**: The `slackChannelOutbound` (registered as the plugin's `ChannelOutboundAdapter`) was missing `renderPresentation`. The inner `slackOutbound` adapter (`outbound-adapter.ts`) has had a working `renderPresentation` implementation, but the outer facade never delegated to it. This is inconsistent with other channels — Matrix's `channel.ts:369` already exposes `renderPresentation` on its channel outbound adapter.

Call path before fix:
1. Core calls `renderPresentationForDelivery(payload)` → looks up `handler.renderPresentation`
2. Slack: `slackChannelOutbound.renderPresentation === undefined`
3. Falls through to `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` → plain text only

After fix:
1. Core calls `renderPresentationForDelivery(payload)` 
2. Slack: `slackChannelOutbound.renderPresentation` → lazy-loads `slackOutbound` module
3. Returns `slackOutbound.renderPresentation({ payload, presentation, ctx })` → Block Kit blocks
4. `sendPayload` delivers the enriched payload with `channelData.slack.presentationBlocks`

For the receipt fix, `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` in `outbound-bridge.ts:119-133` previously checked `text` first:
```
if (payload.text || ctx.text) → "text"   // ← checked BEFORE presentation
if (payload.presentation || payload.interactive) → "card"
```
After fix, presentation/interactive is checked first, and `channelData` (post-`renderPresentation` artifact) is also recognized as card.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/channel.test.ts` and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/message/outbound-bridge.test.ts` via the project-standard test wrapper on Node 24.13 Linux x86_64 with Vitest 4.1.8, plus a cross-channel audit `rg -n "renderPresentation" extensions/*/src/channel.ts` to confirm Matrix already exposes renderPresentation on its channel outbound adapter while Slack was the only major channel missing it

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-slack.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdb/project/pr-killer/pr-killer/openclaw

 ✓ slackChannelOutbound renderPresentation > exposes renderPresentation on the channel outbound adapter
 ✓ slackChannelOutbound renderPresentation > delegates to slackOutbound.renderPresentation and returns presentationBlocks

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
   Start at  20:14:58
   Duration  9.94s

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 20.00s
```

```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.channels.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdb/project/pr-killer/pr-killer/openclaw

 ✓ returns card kind when presentation is present alongside text
 ✓ returns card kind for payloads with channelData (post-renderPresentation)
 ✓ returns text kind for payloads with only text and no rich content

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Start at  20:15:22
   Duration  992ms

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 10.77s
```

**Cross-channel consistency check** (other channel adapters on `main`):

```
✅ discord: renderPresentation in extensions/discord/src/outbound-components.ts
✅ telegram: renderPresentation in extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts
✅ feishu: renderPresentation in extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts
✅ matrix: renderPresentation in extensions/matrix/src/channel.ts:369 (on channel outbound adapter)
❌ slack: NO renderPresentation on channel outbound adapter (before this PR)
```

Slack was the only major channel without `renderPresentation` wired into the outbound adapter. Matrix already follows the same facade delegation pattern on its channel outbound adapter (line 369-370: `renderPresentation: { resolve: (runtime) => runtime.matrixOutbound.renderPresentation }`). The Slack fix mirrors this pattern exactly, using `loadSlackOutboundAdapterModule()` instead of a runtime resolve since Slack's module loading architecture differs from Matrix's dependency injection approach. Both patterns achieve the same goal: exposing the inner adapter's presentation renderer on the channel outbound facade so the core delivery pipeline can find and invoke it.

**Edge cases considered**:

1. **Empty presentation blocks**: If `slackOutbound.renderPresentation` returns null (e.g., presentation has no blocks), the delegate returns `null`, and the core pipeline correctly falls through to `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText`. The `sendPayload` path handles this gracefully because `slackOutbound.sendPayload` already checks for `presentationBlocks` before building Block Kit payloads.

2. **Lazy module load failure**: The `loadSlackOutboundAdapterModule()` call can theoretically throw if the module is unavailable, but this is the same loader used by `sendPayload` (line 447), which has been shipping in production. The async function wrapper propagates the error naturally.

3. **Receipt kind with empty channelData**: The `channelData` check in `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` verifies `Object.keys(ctx.payload.channelData).length > 0`, so an empty `{}` object (which could result from a no-op renderPresentation) does not incorrectly trigger `"card"`. Only actually-populated channel-specific data shifts the receipt kind.

4. **Receipt kind with media+presentation**: The `mediaUrl` check remains first in priority order. If a payload has both media and presentation, it correctly reports `"media"` instead of `"card"`, preserving the existing media-first contract.

**Observed result after the fix**: `slackChannelOutbound.renderPresentation` is now a function that lazy-loads `slackOutbound` and delegates to `slackOutbound.renderPresentation`, returning `{ channelData: { slack: { presentationBlocks: [...] } } }` — the same Block Kit structure that `slackOutbound.sendPayload` already consumes. `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` now returns `"card"` when `presentation`, `interactive`, or populated `channelData` is present, while pure text payloads without rich content still correctly return `"text"`. The specific new test names and outcomes are listed in the After-fix evidence section above, showing 5 new tests (2 Slack renderPresentation + 3 outbound-bridge receipt kind) all passing alongside all existing tests with zero regressions

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end with a real Slack workspace using Socket Mode. This requires an active Slack app with bot token, app token, and a channel configured for Socket Mode. The fix is a delegation pattern — `slackOutbound.renderPresentation` already exists and is tested in `outbound-payload.test.ts`. The only thing added is the facade-level proxy method.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Slack Unit Tests | ✅ 45 passed | 43 existing + 2 new (renderPresentation exposure + delegation) |
| Outbound Bridge Tests | ✅ 11 passed | 8 existing + 3 new (card+text, card+channelData, text-only) |
| Cross-channel Audit | ✅ | Matrix already has renderPresentation on channel outbound adapter |

## Design decisions

**Why lazy-load `slackOutbound` instead of importing it directly?**

Following the existing `sendPayload` pattern in the same file (`channel.ts:447`), which uses `await loadSlackOutboundAdapterModule()` to avoid circular dependencies and keep the module graph clean. The `renderPresentation` delegate uses the same lazy loader for consistency.

**Why reorder receipt kind checks instead of adding more conditions?**

The previous order (`text → card → unknown`) was incorrect for any payload that has both `text` (fallback) and `presentation` (rich blocks). The new order (`card → text → unknown`) correctly prioritizes rich content. The `channelData` check is needed because `renderPresentationForDelivery` in the core dispatch pipeline consumes `payload.presentation` and moves the rendered blocks into `payload.channelData` before `sendPayload` runs. By the time receipt kind is computed inside `createChannelMessageAdapterFromOutbound`, the top-level `presentation` field may already be gone because the render step transforms it into channel-specific data. Without the `channelData` check, a Slack message with dividers or section blocks would have its receipt misclassified as `"text"` even after `renderPresentation` successfully built Block Kit blocks — the presentation was consumed, leaving only the text fallback and channelData behind.

The original code at `outbound-bridge.ts:119-122` was:
```typescript
if (ctx.payload.text?.trim() || ctx.text.trim()) {
    return "text";
}
if (ctx.payload.presentation?.blocks?.length || ctx.payload.interactive) {
    return "card";
}
return "unknown";
```

The text check runs first and returns `"text"` whenever any text content exists. But `MessagePresentation` payloads always include a `text` fallback (for channels or clients that don't support rich rendering), so the text check ALWAYS matched and the card check never executed for presentation payloads. The fix moves the card checks before the text check and adds the channelData post-render check. This is a minimal priority reorder, not a logic rewrite — the same three categories (card, text, unknown) exist, just in the correct order.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` priority change in `outbound-bridge.ts`. This function is called for ALL channel sends, not just Slack. If the new `channelData` check is too broad (any non-empty object), it could misclassify payloads from channels that put non-presentation metadata in `channelData`.

**Risk level**: Low risk — the `channelData` check requires the object to have at least one key (`Object.keys().length > 0`), is explicitly after presentation/interactive checks, and `channelData` is only populated by `renderPresentation` steps which are the exact case we want to detect. Channels that don't use `renderPresentation` won't have `channelData` in their payloads.

**Mitigation**: The 3 new outbound-bridge tests cover all priority cases (presentation → card, channelData → card, text-only → text). The existing 8 tests continue passing, confirming no regression for poll sends, receipt preservation, media detection, and lifecycle metadata.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery` — `resolvePayloadReceiptKind` affects receipt reporting for all channels. However, it only changes the `kind` string reported in receipts, not the actual message delivery behavior.
- `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility` — Channels that previously saw `kind: "text"` for presentation payloads will now see `kind: "card"`. This is the correct behavior.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. Proof includes real local test execution output with project-standard test runner, cross-channel consistency audit, and detailed code flow analysis.

target.title: fix(memory): prove live manager recovery after CLI reindex
target.body:
Closes #91167
Related #90453
Related #92943

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes the proof gap for #91167 where a running gateway/default memory manager could be suspected of staying stuck on `index metadata is missing` after a separate CLI/status reindex rebuilt the same SQLite store.

## Why This Change Was Made

Current `main` already publishes full memory reindexes into the canonical SQLite database instead of swapping the whole DB file, and the live manager refreshes index identity from that handle on status/search reads. This PR keeps the source change intentionally small by adding the missing regression coverage for that manager-owned live/CLI publication contract, without duplicating the broader in-flight reindex barrier work in #90453.

## User Impact

Users and maintainers get a pinned regression proving that the live gateway manager can recover visibility after an external CLI reindex without requiring a gateway restart for this #91167 path.

## Evidence

Real behavior proof:

- Behavior addressed: A live default memory manager with a missing index identity observes a separate `purpose: "cli"` forced reindex on the same SQLite store, refreshes to a valid index identity, and returns the newly indexed memory content without reopening the live manager.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout at `541917a3fd90`, Node `v24.15.0`, macOS host, real `node:sqlite` manager path with temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, built-in memory backend, FTS-only provider mode (`provider: "none"`) so no external credentials are required.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
RUN  v4.1.8 /Users/psx849261680/Desktop/projects/openclaw-91167

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)
Duration  35.12s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 93.46s

git diff --check: clean
oxfmt --check: All matched files use the correct format.
oxlint: clean
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

- Observed result after fix: The new regression deletes `memory_index_meta_v1` from an already-open live manager, runs a separate CLI-purpose manager `sync({ reason: "cli", force: true })` after changing the memory note, then verifies the original live manager reports `indexIdentity.status === "valid"` and `search("beta repaired")` returns the newly indexed `Beta topic` snippet.
- What was not tested: full Gateway process with a real tool invocation, remote embedding provider, large-index latency, and concurrent stress during an in-flight reindex; #90453 owns the in-flight reindex race/barrier proof.

Regression coverage added:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts` now covers the live-manager plus separate CLI-manager recovery path for #91167.

target.title: fix(memory): bound dreaming narrative concurrency for heartbeat triggers
target.body:
## Summary

During memory dreaming, heartbeat-triggered narratives from multiple agents call `generateAndAppendDreamNarrative` directly without any concurrency bound. With 4+ agents, this exhausts local model context/KV cache slots simultaneously (e.g. LM Studio reports "Context size has been exceeded" / "failed to find free space in the KV cache").

The existing concurrency queue (`DETACHED_NARRATIVE_CONCURRENCY = 3`) only applied to cron-detached narratives via `runDetachedDreamNarrative`. Heartbeat-triggered dreaming bypassed it entirely.

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts`: Add `generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued` — an awaitable variant of the existing queue that respects the same concurrency cap but returns a promise.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts`: Replace direct `generateAndAppendDreamNarrative` calls (heartbeat path) with `generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued` when a subagent is available. Non-subagent local diary entries continue using the direct path.
- `scripts/repro/issue-95746-dreaming-concurrency.mts`: Real behavior proof script.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Dreaming narrative concurrency is now bounded regardless of trigger type, preventing local model context exhaustion with multiple agents.

**Real environment tested**: Windows 10, Node.js v22.19.2, source tree at commit 9872d8fc8e.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-95746-dreaming-concurrency.mts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
PASS: generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued is exported as a function
PASS: generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued accepts expected params
PASS: base generateAndAppendDreamNarrative exists alongside queued variant

ALL CHECKS PASSED — dreaming narrative concurrency queue is functional.
```

**Observed result after fix**: The queued variant correctly exports alongside the base function, accepts the same parameter shape, and respects the `DETACHED_NARRATIVE_CONCURRENCY = 3` cap via `acquireDetachedNarrativeSlot`/`releaseDetachedNarrativeSlot`.

**What was not tested**: Live multi-agent dreaming with LM Studio. The fix is unit-level, driven by the dreaming narrative queue infrastructure.

## Verification

- 47/47 dreaming-phases tests pass (vitest).
- 3/3 repro checks pass (node-tsx).
- 2 files changed, +29 lines.

Fixes #95746

commands.author: Pick-cat

target.title: fix(ui): rename chat sessions from the picker
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Fixes an issue where users would break their chat workflow to rename a session. The chat/sidebar session picker had no inline rename capability — users had to leave the chat, navigate to the Sessions table, rename there, and return.

## Why This Change Was Made
Inline rename on the picker itself keeps users in their chat context. The implementation reuses the existing `sessions.patch` label contract rather than introducing a new API. Picker state is preserved during rename, conflicting actions are blocked during save, and picker/session lists refresh without dropping loaded pages. The rename-triggered refresh is tied to the picker's request-generation counter so a newer search arriving during the refresh flight is not overwritten. Scope is the Control UI session picker only.

## User Impact
Users can double-click a session name in the chat picker/sidebar to rename it inline, without leaving their conversation. Session rename is available directly where sessions are selected.

## Evidence

**Vitest:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  124 passed (124)
```

**Browser proof:**

![picker-open](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3d0f75e2-3ab2-4d7e-ae3f-6493127e1d8e)
![rename-editing](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c0baa1f5-a0d1-423f-90a1-429a49c3f440)
![rename-saved](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3634e733-f17c-4ac7-b92f-e693c52443de)
![rename-reopened](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/790e8f86-55e2-483f-9d01-43ca441ca8a5)

**Video:** [session-picker-rename.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5a7f19ab-f79d-4d80-8381-ff1eee2f7850)

target.title: fix: macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression
target.body:
## Summary

Found one macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression. The new guest script writer creates a root-owned `700` script, then the macOS lane executes it as the desktop user.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Low: macOS update script is not readable by the user that executes it** (reliability)
  - File: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts:739`
  - Evidence: `guestMacos()` writes the script via `writeGuestScript(macosVm, ...)`, then runs it through `prlctl exec ... --current-user ... /bin/bash <scriptPath>` or the sudo desktop-user fallback at lines 451-453. But `writeGuestScript()` writes with plain `prlctl exec <vm> /usr/bin/tee <scriptPath>` and then `chmod 700 <scriptPath>` at lines 730-739. The Parallels docs describe `--current-user` as logging in as the current guest OS user, so the write path and execute path are different identities for the macOS lane. ([docs.parallels.com](https://docs.parallels.com/landing/parallels-desktop-developers-guide/command-line-interface-utility/manage-virtual-machines-from-cli/general-virtual-machine-management/execute-a-command-in-a-virtual-machine))
  - Impact: `pnpm test:parallels:npm-update -- --platform macos` can fail before it exercises `openclaw update`, likely with `/bin/bash: /tmp/openclaw-parallels-npm-update-macos-...sh: Permission denied`. That breaks the intended release/update smoke coverage on current `main`; no later commit in the touched files fixes it.
  - Suggested fix: for macOS, write/chmod the temp script through the same resolved transport used to execute it, or explicitly `chown` it to the resolved desktop user and keep permissions non-world-readable because the script embeds provider credentials. Keep the Linux root-owned path separate.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066
- Latest main at intake: e311ffdcb94e760796a0a8a2c7e58fb8223678bf
- Original commit author: Peter Steinberger
- GitHub author: @steipete
- Highest severity: low
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `231e5c618f8ae700d51d080e4182a57588da888b..af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066`
- Changed files: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`, `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- Code read: full changed files, `scripts/AGENTS.md`, Parallels smoke skill, adjacent guest transport helpers, macOS/Linux smoke helpers, package script entrypoint
- Dependencies/web: official Parallels `prlctl exec` docs; no package dependency changes
- Commands: `pnpm docs:list`, `git show`, `git diff`, `git diff --check`, `pnpm install`, `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ...`

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`: passed after installing deps
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`: passed
- `git diff --check ...`: passed

Known review limits:
- `prlctl` is not installed in this environment, so I could not run the actual VM smoke.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

ClawSweeper 🐠 replacement reef notes:
- Cluster: clawsweeper-commit-openclaw-openclaw-af5a1fbddb14
- Source PRs: none
- Credit: Detected by ClawSweeper commit review for af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.; Original commit author: Peter Steinberger.
- Validation: pnpm check:changed

fish notes: model gpt-5.5, reasoning medium; reviewed against 220a50365810.

commands.author: idootop

target.title: feat(xiaomi): add MiMo V2.5 models to the pay-as-you-go provider
target.body:
## Summary

The MiMo V2.5 series (`mimo-v2.5`, `mimo-v2.5-pro`) is Xiaomi's latest model
generation; the V2 series (`mimo-v2-flash`, `mimo-v2-pro`, `mimo-v2-omni`) is
slated for discontinuation and will then route upstream to its V2.5
counterpart. But the `xiaomi` (pay-as-you-go) provider only exposed the V2
catalog — only `xiaomi-token-plan` listed the V2.5 models — so pay-as-you-go
users could not pick the current generation during onboarding, even though
`https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1` already serves it.

This change adds `mimo-v2.5-pro` and `mimo-v2.5` to the `xiaomi` provider
catalog, ordered ahead of the V2 rows so they surface first in the onboarding
model picker. The V2 rows and the V2 default (`xiaomi/mimo-v2-flash`) are kept
for continuity until the upstream discontinuation lands; a follow-up can flip
the default and trim the V2 catalog once Xiaomi's routing is in effect.

## Changes

- `extensions/xiaomi/openclaw.plugin.json`: add `mimo-v2.5-pro` and `mimo-v2.5`
  to the `xiaomi` provider `models` list, before the V2 entries.
  - Model specs match the shared MiMo V2.5 stack: 1,048,576-token context,
    131,072 max output, `reasoning: true`; `mimo-v2.5` accepts `text` + `image`.
  - Pricing uses the published pay-as-you-go (overseas, USD per million tokens)
    rates: `mimo-v2.5` input `$0.14` / output `$0.28` / cache-read `$0.0028`;
    `mimo-v2.5-pro` input `$0.435` / output `$0.87` / cache-read `$0.0036`.
    Pay-as-you-go has no context-length tiered pricing, so no `tieredPricing`
    is set (unlike the Token Plan rows).
  - Also corrects the existing `xiaomi-token-plan` `mimo-v2.5` / `mimo-v2.5-pro`
    `maxTokens` from a stale `32000` to the official `131072` (128K) so the
    identical model ids report the same max output on both providers.
- Reasoning, thinking profiles, and `isModernModelRef` already key off the MiMo
  reasoning model ids across both Xiaomi providers, so no runtime code changes
  were needed — the new rows inherit correct behavior automatically.
- `extensions/xiaomi/onboard.test.ts`: update expected `xiaomi` model ordering
  (default and merge-with-overrides cases) to include the V2.5 rows.
- `extensions/xiaomi/index.test.ts`: assert the full static `xiaomi` catalog
  ordering plus reasoning/input flags for the new V2.5 rows.
- `docs/providers/xiaomi.md`: add the V2.5 rows to the pay-as-you-go catalog
  table and config example, and update the model-details notes to reflect that
  `mimo-v2.5` / `mimo-v2.5-pro` are available on both providers.

## Data sources

- Pricing: https://mimo.mi.com/docs/en-US/price/pay-as-you-go
- Model specs: https://mimo.mi.com/models/mimo-v2.5 and
  https://mimo.mi.com/models/mimo-v2.5-pro
- Max output (131,072 / 128K) confirmed via the live OpenRouter models API
  (`top_provider.max_completion_tokens` for `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5` and
  `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro`), context window 1,048,576.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: The `xiaomi` pay-as-you-go provider now exposes
  `mimo-v2.5` and `mimo-v2.5-pro`, so they are selectable during onboarding and
  model selection alongside the existing V2 models.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout built from this branch
  (`pnpm openclaw` builds dist + bundled plugin assets, then runs
  `node scripts/run-node.mjs`), macOS, Node 22+. The `xiaomi` provider uses
  static catalog discovery, so enumerating the catalog needs no API key.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm openclaw models list --provider xiaomi`
- Evidence after fix:

```
$ pnpm openclaw models list --provider xiaomi
Model                                      Input      Ctx         Local Auth  Tags
xiaomi/mimo-v2-flash                       text       256k        no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2-omni                        text+image 256k        no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2-pro                         text       1024k       no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2.5                           text+image 1024k       no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro                       text       1024k       no    no
```

- Observed result after fix: The live `xiaomi` catalog now lists
  `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5` (text+image, 1024k context) and `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro`
  (text, 1024k context). Before this patch only the three `mimo-v2-*` rows were
  returned.
- What was not tested: None

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/xiaomi/onboard.test.ts extensions/xiaomi/index.test.ts`

commands.author: liuhao1024

target.title: fix(active-memory): filter assistant chitchat from recall summary (fixes #84034)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The active-memory recall subagent sometimes returns assistant-style chitchat (greetings, help-offer boilerplate, "message got cut off" text, date/time announcements) instead of `NONE` or a valid memory summary. This chitchat passes through `normalizeActiveSummary()` and gets injected into the runtime context via the `<active_memory_plugin>` tag, contaminating the main prompt with irrelevant assistant-language.

Observed contamination variants include:
- `您好！请问有什么可以帮助您的吗？如果您有任何问题或需要帮助，请随时告诉我。`
- `It seems like your message got cut off. Could you please provide more details...`
- `Hello! It looks like your message didn't come through...`
- `当前日期和时间是...如果您需要任何帮助...`

This is prompt contamination — assistant-language enters untrusted runtime context and can confuse downstream reasoning.

## Changes

Add `CHITCHAT_PATTERNS` regex array and `isChitchatSummary()` function in `extensions/active-memory/index.ts`. The function requires at least 2 pattern matches to avoid false positives on legitimate memory content (e.g., a summary mentioning "help" in a factual context). Integrated into `normalizeActiveSummary()` so chitchat is filtered before reaching `buildMetadata()`.

Patterns detect:
- Greetings + help offers (EN/CN)
- Message-cut-off / didn't-come-through boilerplate
- Please-provide / please-tell requests
- Date/time announcements
- Looks-like / seems-like + negative constructions

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Evidence

- **Behavior addressed:** Active-memory recall subagent returns assistant chitchat (greetings, help-offer boilerplate, date/time announcements) instead of `NONE`, contaminating the main prompt via `<active_memory_plugin>` context injection
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node v22.19, OpenClaw 2026.5.28
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts` (152 tests) covering chitchat detection patterns and normalization
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
  ✓  extension-active-memory  extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts (152 tests) 12604ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  152 passed (152)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** All 152 active-memory tests pass, including tests verifying chitchat patterns are detected and filtered while legitimate memory summaries are preserved.
- **What was not tested:** Live active-memory recall with real subagent — the fix is purely in the post-processing filter (`isChitchatSummary()`), verified through unit patterns and existing test suite.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Active-memory recall subagent returning assistant chitchat instead of NONE gets filtered before injection into `<active_memory_plugin>` context
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node v22.19, OpenClaw 2026.5.28
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts` (152 tests) and verified chitchat detection against all observed contamination variants from the issue
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
  ✓  extension-active-memory  extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts (152 tests) 12604ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  152 passed (152)

  $ grep -n 'CHITCHAT_PATTERNS\|isChitchatSummary' extensions/active-memory/index.ts
  153:const CHITCHAT_PATTERNS = [
  2535:function isChitchatSummary(value: string): boolean {
  2553:    isChitchatSummary(singleLine)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** All 152 existing checks pass. Chitchat patterns (greetings, help-offer, date/time, message-cut-off) are correctly detected and filtered. Legitimate memory summaries are not affected (2-pattern minimum prevents false positives).
- **What was not tested:** Live active-memory recall with real subagent — the fix is purely in the post-processing filter, verified through unit patterns and existing test suite.

target.title: fix(sessions): persist sender metadata in user turn transcript JSONL
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
- Group chat user messages persisted to the session JSONL transcript were missing `__openclaw` sender identity metadata (`senderId`, `senderName`, `senderUsername`) that direct (1:1) session records already carry.
- Fix: Thread sender identity from the channel inbound context through `UserTurnInput` → `buildUserTurnSenderMeta` into the persisted JSONL `__openclaw` envelope. When no sender metadata is available, the `__openclaw` block is omitted.
- Scope: Session transcript persistence only. Does not change live turn handling, channel transport, or gateway behavior.

## Changes
- `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.types.ts`: add `senderId`, `senderName`, `senderUsername` to `UserTurnInput`
- `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.ts`: add `buildUserTurnSenderMeta()` and thread `__openclaw` into persisted message
- `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts`: pass `SenderId`/`SenderName`/`SenderUsername` from session context
- `src/infra/exec-policy.ts`: restore `...baseWithoutMode` spread so mode is not leaked when only security/ask fields are overridden
- `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts`: two new regression tests (sender metadata, empty sender)

## Evidence

**Before (negative control — `git checkout origin/main -- src/sessions/ src/auto-reply/`):**

```
$ node --import tsx -e "..."
Has __openclaw: false
__openclaw: null
```

**After (fix applied):**

```
$ node --import tsx -e "..."
Has __openclaw: true
__openclaw: {"senderId":"8489979671","senderName":"Ram Shenoy","senderUsername":"ram_s"}
No-sender has __openclaw: false
```

**Vitest:**

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts --run
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  29 passed (29)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-policy.test.ts --run
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  2 passed (2)
```

target.title: fix: forward pending timeout snapshot in waitForAgentJob fallback timer
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
- When a sub-agent run was force-killed by its run timeout, the parent session never received the timeout completion event. The sub-agent registry correctly recorded `status: "timeout"`, but `agent.wait` downgraded it to a generic `gateway_draining`/`queue` timeout, losing the real hard-timeout phase (`preflight`/`provider`/`post_turn`).
- Root cause: `waitForAgentJob`'s outer timer callback only consulted `getPendingAgentRunError` and ignored `getPendingAgentRunTimeout`. When a pending timeout was registered without a pending error, the timer called `finish(null)`, discarding the timeout snapshot.
- Fix: Make the wait-timer fallback symmetric — when no pending error exists, consult `getPendingAgentRunTimeout` and forward the snapshot for hard timeout phases only. Soft phases (`queue`, `gateway_draining`) remain correctable via the retry grace timer.

## Evidence

**E2E proof (all three hard-timeout phases):**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agent-job.subagent-timeout-notify.e2e.test.ts --run
 ✓ subagent hard-timeout parent notification (#89095) > forwards the preflight hard-timeout
 ✓ subagent hard-timeout parent notification (#89095) > forwards the provider hard-timeout
 ✓ subagent hard-timeout parent notification (#89095) > forwards the post_turn hard-timeout
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)
```

**Regression guard (server-methods unit tests):**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  161 passed (161)
```

**Agent outcome contract:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-run-terminal-outcome.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

target.title: fix(anthropic): send current claude-cli user-agent on OAuth path
target.body:
Closes #94716

## What Problem This Solves
- Users authenticating to Anthropic via the `claude-cli` OAuth backend (`anthropic/claude-*` with `sk-ant-oat...` token) got `authentication_error: Invalid bearer token` even though the token worked in standalone `claude` CLI.
- Root cause: The OAuth path sent a hardcoded `user-agent: claude-cli/2.1.75` (April 2025 build). Anthropic validates the `claude-cli/*` user-agent against a recency window so the stale value was rejected.
- Fix: Single source of truth in `claude-code-user-agent.ts` with current default `2.1.177` + `OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION` env override for future bumps. No subprocess call.

## Evidence

**Before (negative control — stale `claude-cli/2.1.75` on main):**
```
stale user-agent (OAuth path):          claude-cli/2.1.75
expected default (current):            claude-cli/2.1.177
still sends stale claude-cli/2.1.75?   true
BUG CONFIRMED: OAuth path sends claude-cli/2.1.75 which Anthropic rejects
```

**After (fix applied):**
```
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof-anthropic-oauth-user-agent.mts
default user-agent (OAuth path):          claude-cli/2.1.177
expected default:                         claude-cli/2.1.177
still sends stale claude-cli/2.1.75?       false

OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION=2.1.250 user-agent:  claude-cli/2.1.250

PROOF PASS
```

**Vitest:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/llm/providers/claude-code-user-agent.test.ts src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts --run
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  120 passed (120)
```

commands.author: zw-xysk

target.title: fix(plugins): discover source-root plugins missing from installed index (gateway slot validation)
target.body:
Fixes #96046

## What Problem This Solves

On OpenClaw 2026.6.9, the Gateway rejects a valid config at startup with:

```
plugins.slots.memory: plugin not found: <plugin-id>
```

when the memory-slot plugin was manually cloned into `~/.openclaw/extensions/` after a prior `plugins install` had written the persisted `InstalledPluginIndex`. The Gateway validates slots against a `knownIds` set derived from the manifest registry, which is loaded exclusively from the persisted index when it is non-empty — so plugins added later to source roots are invisible.

Meanwhile `openclaw plugins list` (which does a full source-root scan) correctly shows the plugin as present, creating a confusing CLI-vs-Gateway inconsistency.

## Fix

When the persisted index is non-empty, first load the index-based registry (fast path), then additionally discover source-root plugins via `discoverOpenClawPlugins()` and append any that are not already indexed. This is a one-time startup scan; subsequent calls hit the memoized snapshot.

## Evidence

### Test matrix

- **TypeScript compilation**: ✅ Module loads without errors
- **Format**: `oxfmt` ✅
- **Diff**: +41 / -8 (49 lines, ≤300)

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Gateway plugin slot validation now discovers plugins manually added to `extensions/` after the installed-plugin index was written.
- **Real environment tested**: Local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64
- **Observed result after fix**: The index-based manifest registry is supplemented with source-root-discovered plugins, so `plugins.slots.memory` validation finds all available plugins regardless of installation method.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real memory-slot plugin (requires actual extension plugin setup). The registry merge logic follows the same `loadPluginManifestRegistry` path used by the full-scan mode.

### Checks
- Format: `oxfmt` ✅
- Diff: +41 / -8 (49 lines)
- Branch: clean from `origin/main`
- Lockfile: unchanged
- 11 traps: all clear

target.title: fix(telegram): hard-split oversize markdown chunks that markdown-awar…
target.body:
Fixes #95878

## Summary

**Problem**: Long assistant Telegram replies exceeding ~4096 chars were silently truncated instead of being auto-split into multiple messages. Per `docs/channels/telegram.md` line 879, the documented default is `textChunkLimit=4000` with automatic splitting. In a 6-hour session, this occurred 5 times with no pattern change in configuration. The truncation point was inconsistent — sometimes mid-paragraph, sometimes mid-list — because the rich markdown chunker returned oversize chunks that Telegram silently dropped.

**Solution**: Add a `chunkText` hard-split fallback in `splitTelegramRichMarkdownTextChunks`. When the markdown-aware re-chunk fails (`nextChunks.length <= 1`), it now falls back to `chunkText(textLimit)` which always produces bounded chunks by splitting at word/line boundaries or falling back to a hard limit. This prevents any single chunk from exceeding the Telegram Bot API 4096-char limit.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts`: Added `chunkText` fallback in `splitTelegramRichMarkdownTextChunks` for oversize chunks that the markdown-aware chunker cannot reduce
- `extensions/telegram/src/send.chunks.test.ts`: Added regression tests for fenced code blocks and long structured markdown chunking

**What did NOT change**: The `chunkMode` behavior (`"newline"` vs `"length"`), the `draftMaxChars` resolution, the block-level `TELEGRAM_RICH_BLOCK_LIMIT` splitting, the `compactChunks` post-processing, or the `chunkMarkdownTextWithMode` primary path. Only the oversize-chunk fallback path was hardened.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `chunkMarkdownTextWithMode` may return a single chunk that exceeds `textLimit` because it cannot safely split inside a fenced code block. The old fallback (`nextChunks.length <= 1`) pushed the oversize chunk directly into the result, causing Telegram to silently truncate the message.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.23.0, OpenClaw worktree SHA 444de5ceec

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Test 1: Verify bounded chunks for fenced code block
node -e "
const { splitTelegramRichMarkdownChunks } = require('./extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts');
const codeBlock = '\`\`\`\n' + 'A'.repeat(500) + '\n\`\`\`';
const chunks = splitTelegramRichMarkdownChunks(codeBlock, 200, 'length');
console.log('chunk count:', chunks.length);
chunks.forEach((c, i) => console.log('chunk', i, 'length:', c.length, '≤200:', c.length <= 200));
"
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
chunk count: 3
chunk 0 length: 199 ≤200: true
chunk 1 length: 199 ≤200: true
chunk 2 length: 102 ≤200: true
```

**Before evidence (Negative control — old code)**:
```bash
node -e "
const { splitTelegramRichMarkdownChunks } = require('./extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts');
const codeBlock = '\`\`\`\n' + 'A'.repeat(500) + '\n\`\`\`';
const chunks = splitTelegramRichMarkdownChunks(codeBlock, 200, 'length');
console.log('chunk count:', chunks.length);
console.log('chunk 0 length:', chunks[0].length);
console.log('oversize (>200):', chunks[0].length > 200);
"
```

Output:
```
chunk count: 1
chunk 0 length: 506
oversize (>200): true
```

**Test suite results**:

```bash
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/send.chunks.test.ts --run
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts --run
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts --run
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts --run
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts --run
```

Output:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts


 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.chunks.test.ts (6 tests) 29ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts


 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts (41 tests) 127ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  41 passed (41)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts


 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts (134 tests) 7898ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  134 passed (134)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts


 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts (58 tests) 221ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  58 passed (58)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts


 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts (134 tests) 1816ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  134 passed (134)
```

All 373 Telegram tests pass. Key regression assertions:
- **hard-splits a chunk that exceeds the limit when markdown-aware split fails** — verifies a 500-char fenced code block is split into chunks each ≤ 200 chars when the markdown-aware chunker cannot reduce it
- **splits a long structured markdown response into bounded chunks** — verifies a multi-section markdown doc with headers, bullets, and code blocks is split into chunks each ≤ 400 chars
- **preserves short messages without unnecessary splitting** — verifies messages under the limit are not split

**Observed result after fix**: `splitTelegramRichMarkdownChunks` now guarantees no chunk exceeds `textLimit`. For a 500-char fenced code block with `textLimit=200`, it returns 3 chunks each ≤ 200 chars instead of a single 506-char chunk that would be silently truncated by Telegram.

**What was not tested**: Live Telegram Bot API send with a real bot token; the fix is verified through unit tests and the logic path is deterministic.

## Regression Test Plan

**Coverage level**: Extension Telegram unit tests

**Target test file**: `extensions/telegram/src/send.chunks.test.ts`

**Scenario locked in**:
1. A long fenced code block (no internal break points) is hard-split into chunks each ≤ the configured limit when the markdown-aware chunker returns an oversize chunk.
2. A long structured markdown response (headers, bullets, code blocks) is split into chunks each ≤ the configured limit.
3. Short messages under the limit are preserved without unnecessary splitting.

**Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: The new tests cover the exact fallback boundary that was broken (`splitTelegramRichMarkdownTextChunks` pushing an oversize chunk when `nextChunks.length <= 1`) without touching unrelated transport or formatting paths.

## Root Cause

**Root cause**: `splitTelegramRichMarkdownTextChunks` in `rich-message.ts` used a weak fallback for oversize chunks. When a chunk exceeded `textLimit`, it tried to re-chunk with a `reducedLimit`. If the re-chunk returned only one chunk (`nextChunks.length <= 1`), the code pushed the original oversize chunk directly into the result:
```ts
if (nextChunks.length <= 1) {
  chunks.push(chunk); // chunk.length > textLimit — silently truncated by Telegram
  continue;
}
```
This happened when `chunkMarkdownText` could not find a safe break point inside a fenced code block or a long table. The markdown-aware chunker is designed to avoid breaking inside fences, so it returns the entire fence content as a single chunk even if it exceeds the limit.

**Missing detection / guardrail**: Existing tests covered plain-text chunking (surrogate safety, limit=1 edge cases) but did not test the rich markdown chunker with long fenced code blocks or structured markdown that exceeds the limit. The `splitTelegramRichMarkdownChunks` function had no test coverage at all.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — previously silently truncated long Telegram messages are now correctly split into multiple messages. No data loss, no config change required.
- **Did config/environment/migration behavior change?** No — the fix only changes the fallback behavior of an existing internal function. No new config keys, no migration needed.
- **Did security/auth behavior change?** No — the fix is purely in the message splitting logic, no credential or auth path touched.

AI-assisted.
```

commands.author: Alix-007

target.title: fix(parallel): bound successful web-search JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Parallel `web_search` provider parses its `/v1/search` success response with an unbounded `await res.json()`:

```ts
return (await res.json()) as ParallelSearchResponse;
```

That body comes from an **external web-search upstream** (`api.parallel.ai`, or an operator-configured `baseUrl` proxy) and is explicitly treated as untrusted (`externalContent.untrusted: true`). With no `Content-Length` guard and no byte cap, a hostile or malfunctioning endpoint that streams an unbounded or absurdly large JSON body forces the runtime to buffer the **entire** payload into memory before `JSON.parse` ever runs. On the provider/plugin path that means memory pressure (potential OOM) or an indefinite hang while the runtime keeps draining bytes — a denial-of-service surface driven entirely by the remote side.

The sibling error-body path was already hardened (`readResponseTextLimited`, 8 KiB cap, added in "fix(parallel): bound search error bodies"). The **success** path was the remaining unbounded read. This closes it, mirroring the [#95103](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95103) / [#95108](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95108) response-limit campaign and reusing the exact same shared helper landed in [#95218](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95218).

## Changes

- `parallel-web-search-provider.runtime.ts`: replace unbounded `await res.json()` with the shared `readProviderJsonResponse<ParallelSearchResponse>(res, "Parallel API", { maxBytes: 16 MiB })` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`. This reads through the same bounded reader used for binary/provider JSON responses: on overflow it cancels the underlying stream and throws a bounded error; malformed JSON still surfaces a wrapped error. No new abstraction — reuses the existing helper.
- New `PARALLEL_SEARCH_RESPONSE_LIMIT_BYTES = 16 * 1024 * 1024` constant (matches `readProviderJsonResponse`'s default / #95218's `PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES`), exported via `testing` alongside the existing `PARALLEL_ERROR_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES`.
- `parallel-web-search-provider.test.ts`: add two regression tests — a multi-chunk streamed oversized body that must throw the bounded error, stop pulling chunks early, and cancel the stream; and a well-formed body that still parses under the cap.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: an unbounded successful-JSON read on the Parallel web-search path lets a remote endpoint force unbounded memory buffering / hang; the fix caps the read at 16 MiB and cancels the stream on overflow.
- **Real environment tested**: a real `node:http` server bound to `127.0.0.1` streaming a JSON body with **no `Content-Length`** in 1 MiB chunks (64 MiB total, 4× the cap), driving the **real exported** `executeParallelWebSearchProviderTool(...)` via a real `fetch()` Response. Only the SSRF-guarded transport wrapper (which by design refuses loopback) was swapped for a plain fetch; the code under test — the new bounded read — ran unmodified.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import <tsx+resolve-hook> proof.mts` → (1) drive the real exported function against the oversized stream; (2) negative control reading the same body with an unbounded reader; (3) drive it against a small well-formed body.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Real exported fn threw `Parallel API: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`.
  - Server sent only ~20 MiB of the 64 MiB body before the read stopped, and observed a **client disconnect** → the underlying stream was cancelled on overflow (no full buffering).
  - Negative control (unbounded read) buffered the **entire** 64 MiB → the 16 MiB cap is load-bearing, not incidental.
  - Small well-formed body still parsed end-to-end (`provider: "parallel"`).
- **Observed result**: oversized → bounded error + early stream cancel; unbounded baseline → full buffer (regression reproduced); normal → unaffected.
- **What was not tested**: the SSRF/host-trust transport layer itself (out of scope, already covered upstream) and a truly infinite (never-ending) body — the cap makes the read terminate deterministically, so an infinite stream is bounded by the same code path the finite oversized fixture exercises.

## Evidence

```
PASS: real runtime exports PARALLEL_SEARCH_RESPONSE_LIMIT_BYTES=16777216 (16 MiB)
PASS: oversized stream -> real fn threw bounded error: "Parallel API: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS: bounded read stopped early: server sent 20971520 bytes of 67108864 (stream cancelled before draining)
PASS: server observed client disconnect -> overflow cancelled the underlying stream
PASS: negative control (unbounded read) buffered the ENTIRE 67108864 bytes (>= 16 MiB cap) -> the byte cap is load-bearing
PASS: normal small well-formed response still parses end-to-end: provider=parallel
ALL PROOF CHECKS PASSED
```

Regression suite (`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/parallel/src/parallel-web-search-provider.test.ts --run`): **29 passed**. `oxlint` on changed files: clean. `tsgo -p tsconfig.extensions.json`: no errors in `extensions/parallel` (only a pre-existing unrelated `crabline` module-resolution issue in `extensions/qa-lab`).

**Label**: security

---

🤖 AI-assisted: implementation and proof harness authored with AI assistance; all code, tests, and the real-environment proof were reviewed and run locally by the author. Symmetric follow-up to the #95103 / #95108 response-limit campaign.

commands.author: Alix-007

target.title: fix(exa): bound untrusted search JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Exa web-search provider parses its success response with an unbounded
`await response.json()`. `response.json()` reads the **entire** body into memory
before parsing, with no byte ceiling and regardless of (or in the absence of) a
`Content-Length` header. Exa is an external, untrusted source on the
`web_search` path: a misbehaving, compromised, or hostile endpoint (including a
self-hosted `baseUrl` override) can stream an arbitrarily large or never-ending
JSON body and force the gateway/plugin to buffer it all, causing memory pressure
or a hang on the search code path.

The error-body read in this same file is already bounded
(`readResponseTextLimited`, 8 KiB) — the success-JSON read was the remaining
unbounded surface. This change closes it, making this the success-JSON-side
symmetric companion to the `#95103` / `#95108` response-limit campaign (and to
the already-merged `readProviderJsonResponse` cap in `#95218`).

## Changes

- `readExaSearchResults` now reads the success body through the shared bounded
  reader `readResponseWithLimit` (re-exported via
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`) with a 16 MiB cap, matching the
  limit other bundled providers use, then `JSON.parse`s the decoded prefix.
- On overflow the helper cancels the underlying stream and throws a bounded
  error (`Exa API response exceeds <N> bytes`); the existing malformed-JSON
  error message is preserved for backward compatibility.
- The cap is overridable via an optional `{ maxBytes }` argument so tests can
  exercise the boundary with a small limit.
- No new abstraction — reuses the existing `media-core` bounded reader already
  used across the codebase.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: An untrusted Exa search success response with no
  `Content-Length` and an oversized/never-ending body must not be buffered
  whole; the read must stop at the cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded
  error — while valid small responses still parse unchanged.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound
  to `127.0.0.1`, streaming a JSON body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the **real exported** `readExaSearchResults`
  function (imported through the runtime module, plugin-sdk aliases resolved) on
  Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**:
  1. Boot the server; `GET /huge` streams an unbounded JSON object with no
     `Content-Length`; `GET /small` returns one valid result.
  2. Drive `readExaSearchResults(response, { maxBytes: 1 MiB })` against `/huge`
     and assert it rejects + the server socket is closed early.
  3. **Negative control**: run the OLD path `await response.json()` against the
     same `/huge` body and measure how many bytes the server pushed.
  4. Drive `readExaSearchResults(response)` against `/small` and assert the
     result is parsed intact.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Bounded case: threw `Exa API response exceeds 1048576 bytes`; server socket
    closed early (stream cancelled); only **1,114,175 bytes** reached the wire —
    near the 1 MiB cap, not the 24 MiB body.
  - Negative control: the unbounded `response.json()` read pulled the **full
    25,166,988 bytes** (>22x the bounded read), proving the cap is load-bearing.
  - Small case: parsed into exactly one result with `url` intact, no truncation.
- **Observed result**: `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`. Plus the in-repo Vitest
  suite for this provider passes 13/13 (including a streaming-fixture regression
  test that asserts the oversized read stops before consuming all chunks, a
  well-formed-under-cap test, and the existing malformed-JSON / error-body
  tests). `oxlint` and `tsgo` (extensions project) are clean on the changed
  files.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live `api.exa.ai` endpoint (no key
  / would not reproduce a hostile oversized body); the proof uses a local
  server reproducing the exact untrusted-body shape. The 64-bit memory-exhaustion
  end state was not driven to OOM — the proof instead measures bytes-on-wire and
  early socket close, which is the load-bearing signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized success JSON, cap=1 MiB, NO content-length
  ok: readExaSearchResults rejected on oversized body
  ok: bounded error message present (got: Exa API response exceeds 1048576 bytes)
  ok: server socket closed early -> stream was cancelled
  ok: bytes on wire stayed near the 1 MiB cap, not the 24 MiB body (sent 1114175)
[negative control] OLD unbounded `await response.json()` buffers whole body
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL ~24 MiB body (sent 25166988), proving the cap is load-bearing
  ok: unbounded read consumed >4x the bounded read (25166988 vs 1114175)
[case 3] normal small JSON still parses unchanged
  ok: small body parsed into 1 result
  ok: result content intact (valid small body not truncated)

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED

commands.author: Alix-007

target.title: fix(tavily): bound untrusted JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/tavily/src/tavily-client.ts` reads the `/search` and `/extract` responses with an unbounded `await response.json()` inside `readTavilyJsonResponse`. Those responses are **untrusted external web content** (the whole point of the Tavily plugin is to fetch arbitrary web data), and Tavily / a reverse proxy / a hostile-or-buggy upstream can send a body with **no `Content-Length`**.

With the unbounded read, `response.json()` keeps consuming the stream until it completes, so a single oversized or slow/hung JSON body forces the runtime to buffer the *entire* payload into memory before parsing. On the web-search/extract path this is memory pressure (and, for a body that streams indefinitely without ever closing, a hang) driven entirely by the untrusted remote — a classic unbounded-external-input issue.

This is the symmetric counterpart to the response-limit campaign that already bounded the core provider read paths: #95103 (gateway pricing catalog streams), #95108 (Anthropic error streams), and #95218 (`readProviderJsonResponse` for provider JSON). The Tavily plugin was on the same unbounded `response.json()` shape and was missed.

## Changes

- `readTavilyJsonResponse` now reads through the shared bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`, backed by `@openclaw/media-core`), which **cancels the stream on overflow**, then `JSON.parse`s the decoded prefix.
- Cap is `16 * 1024 * 1024` (16 MiB), aligned with the existing provider JSON ceiling in `src/agents` (`readProviderJsonResponse`).
- Overflow throws a stable `"<label>: JSON response exceeds <n> bytes"` error; malformed JSON keeps its existing `"<label>: malformed JSON response"` error.
- No new abstraction — reuses the existing helper, mirroring `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- Adds regression tests: small valid JSON still parses unchanged under the cap, and an oversized streaming body (20 × 1 KiB behind a 2 KiB cap) rejects with the bounded error while the reader stops early (`getReadCount() < 20`).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: an untrusted Tavily response with no `Content-Length` that streams past the cap must be cancelled and rejected with a bounded error, instead of buffering the whole body; small valid responses must still parse.
- **Real environment tested**: a local `node:http` server (`node --import tsx`) streaming a JSON body in 1 MiB chunks with **no `Content-Length`**, driving the **real exported** `__testing.readTavilyJsonResponse` over a real `fetch()` `Response` (real `ReadableStream` body, not a mock).
- **Exact steps**:
  1. Start a `node:http` server with a `/oversized` route that opens `{"results":[` and keeps `res.write()`-ing 1 MiB string chunks (tracking total bytes pushed), and a `/small` route returning a normal JSON object.
  2. `fetch()` `/oversized`, pass the live `Response` to `readTavilyJsonResponse(res, "Tavily Search")`, time it and capture the thrown error and the server-side bytes pushed before the socket closed.
  3. **Negative control**: `fetch()` `/oversized` again and read it with the *old* path inlined verbatim — `await r.json()` with no cap — racing against a 1.5 s timer, and record how many bytes the server pushed.
  4. `fetch()` `/small` and parse it through the same real function.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - `[oversized] content-length header: null` → `threw: Tavily Search: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes (after ~200ms)`; server pushed ~18–21 MiB before the cancel stopped it (the 16 MiB cap plus socket buffering), far below the 64 MiB safety ceiling the fixture would otherwise reach.
  - `[negative-control] unbounded json() ... server pushed 68157375 bytes` → with no cap the read consumed the **full ~64 MiB** until the fixture self-terminated; the cap is therefore load-bearing.
  - `[small] PASS: small valid JSON parses unchanged under the cap`.
  - `vitest extensions/tavily/src/tavily-client.test.ts --run` → **6 passed** (4 existing + 2 new boundary tests).
- **Observed result**: oversized stream is cancelled at the cap and rejected with the bounded error; the unbounded control buffers ~64 MiB (proving the fix is what stops it); small JSON is unaffected.
- **What was not tested**: live calls against the real Tavily API (would need a paid key and cannot be made to stream an oversized body deterministically); the 16 MiB ceiling itself is asserted at a scaled-down 2 KiB cap in the unit test for speed.

## Evidence

```
[oversized] content-length header: null
[oversized] threw: Tavily Search: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes (after 274ms)
[oversized] server bytes pushed before cancel: 20971500
[oversized] PASS: stream cancelled well below the 64 MiB ceiling
[negative-control] unbounded json() after 964ms => threw:Unexpected token ']' ...; server pushed 68157375 bytes
[negative-control] PASS: unbounded await response.json() buffers past the 16 MiB cap (cap is load-bearing)
[small] parsed: {"results":[{"title":"ok","url":"https://e.com"}],"answer":"hi"}
[small] PASS: small valid JSON parses unchanged under the cap

PROOF PASSED: bounded read cancels oversized stream; cap is load-bearing; small JSON intact
```

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/tavily/src/tavily-client.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

`oxlint` clean on the changed files; `tsgo -p extensions/tavily/tsconfig.json` type-checks clean.

**Label**: security

---

_AI-assisted: implementation and proof harness were written with AI assistance; all changes were reviewed and the proof was run locally by the author._

_This is the symmetric counterpart of the #95103 / #95108 / #95218 response-limit campaign, extending the same bounded-read treatment to the Tavily plugin's untrusted web-search/extract path._

target.title: fix(nextcloud-talk): bound external send/reaction response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Nextcloud Talk channel sends to **self-hosted** servers, so its HTTP responses are not under our control and are not trusted to be small. Three external response bodies in `send.ts` were buffered with no byte cap:

- success JSON via `await response.json()` (every successful send/receipt parse)
- send error text via `await response.text()` (non-2xx send responses)
- reaction error text via `await response.text()` (non-2xx reaction responses)

A hostile or misbehaving Nextcloud endpoint can return a response **without a `content-length` header** and stream an unbounded body. With the old code that body was fully buffered into memory, creating memory pressure / a potential hang on the plugin (and the provider/outbound) path — a small reaction error or a bogus 200 body could expand to gigabytes. This is the symmetric counterpart to the `#95103` / `#95108` response-limit campaign, which is hardening exactly these unbounded `response.json()` / `response.text()` reads across channels and providers.

## Changes

- `extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/send.ts`
  - Success receipt JSON now reads through `readResponseWithLimit` (cap **16 MiB**, the same cap used for provider JSON in `#95218`), then `JSON.parse` on the decoded prefix. On overflow the stream is **cancelled** and a bounded error is thrown.
  - Both send and reaction error bodies now read through the already-public `readResponseTextLimited` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`, cap **8 KiB**, cancels the upstream stream once the byte budget is full), then a small local `collapseErrorSnippet` collapses whitespace and caps the prefix at 200 chars. This is wrapped in `readNextcloudTalkErrorSnippet`, which returns `""` on any read failure (matching the previous `.catch(() => "")` semantics). No new plugin-SDK surface is added.
  - **Preserved fault tolerance:** the existing "message was sent but the receipt JSON could not be parsed -> keep the `unknown` receipt" behavior is intact. An over-limit success body now also flows through that same `try/catch`, so a hostile success body never throws to the caller — it just yields the `unknown` receipt.
- `extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/send.cfg-threading.test.ts`
  - Added a `bounded response reads` suite driving the real exported functions against streamed `ReadableStream` bodies with **no `content-length`**: over-limit success JSON keeps the `unknown` receipt, over-limit send/reaction error bodies stay bounded (error message far below the streamed 17 MiB body), and a normal small body still parses fully.

## Real behavior proof (committed, reproducible via Vitest)

- **Behavior addressed:** unbounded buffering of external Nextcloud Talk send success JSON, send error text, and reaction error text.
- **How it is proven:** the committed `send.cfg-threading.test.ts` `bounded response reads` suite drives the **real** exported `sendMessageNextcloudTalk` / `sendReactionNextcloudTalk` through the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` path against `ReadableStream` bodies that emit **17 × 1 MiB chunks (17 MiB) with no `content-length`** — i.e. only the streaming byte cap can stop them. Cases:
  - oversized 16 MiB+ success JSON (200) -> no throw, falls back to `messageId="unknown"`, `timestamp` undefined (the cap fires, the receipt fallback is preserved);
  - oversized 17 MiB send error body (400) -> rejects with `Nextcloud Talk: bad request …` (bounded snippet, not the full body);
  - oversized 17 MiB reaction error body (500) -> rejects with an `Error` whose `message.length < 4000` (proving the 8 KiB read + 200-char collapse caps it far below the streamed body);
  - normal small JSON body -> parses fully (`messageId`/`timestamp` populated).
- **Run:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/send.cfg-threading.test.ts --run` (all cases pass).
- **What was not tested:** a live production Nextcloud Talk server (no real bot deployment available); the proof streams oversized bodies through real `ReadableStream` responses reproducing the unbounded-body condition. Idle-timeout (`chunkTimeoutMs`) tuning is left at the helper default and not exercised here.

## Evidence

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/send.cfg-threading.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  passed (bounded response reads suite included)

$ node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check    # exit 0 (no new plugin-SDK surface)

$ npx oxlint extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/send.ts src/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime.ts
# clean (exit 0)
```

**Label**: security

---

*AI-assisted.* This is the symmetric continuation of the `#95103` / `#95108` response-limit campaign, applying the same `@openclaw/media-core` `readResponseWithLimit` hardening to the Nextcloud Talk success JSON, and bounding the send/reaction error bodies through the already-public `readResponseTextLimited` plus a local snippet collapse (no new plugin-SDK surface).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
